query: Given a protein sequence, can it be determined whether or not it contains a nuclear export signal (NES)? If it does, how strong is the NES, and what is the confidence in that assessment??

schema.explanation: This helps determine if a protein contains a nuclear export signal by analyzing its amino acid sequence.
This helps identify potential NESs by matching the sequence to known NES patterns, such as the LX2-4X2-3XLXL motif.
This provides a confidence score for the predicted NES, indicating the likelihood that the sequence actually contains a functional NES.
This measures the strength of binding between the predicted NES and its corresponding exportin, such as CRM1.
This assesses the ability of the protein to be exported from the nucleus, which can be influenced by the presence and strength of an NES.
This indicates whether the nuclear export of the protein is dependent on the CRM1 exportin, which is a common receptor for NES-containing proteins.
This quantifies the strength of the NES, which can be used to predict the efficiency of nuclear export.
This describes the spacing pattern of hydrophobic residues within the NES, which is important for CRM1 binding and nuclear export.
This predicts the binding conformation of the NES to CRM1, which can affect the efficiency of nuclear export.
This indicates whether the predicted NES is functional and able to facilitate nuclear export.
This predicts the subcellular localization of the protein, which can be influenced by the presence and strength of an NES.
This measures the length of the predicted NES, which can affect its functionality and binding to exportins.
This assesses the conservation of the NES across different species, which can indicate its importance and functionality.
This indicates the specificity of the NES for different exportins, such as CRM1 or calreticulin.
This measures the rate of nuclear export of the protein, which can be influenced by the strength and functionality of the NES.
This categorizes the NES into different classes, such as classical or non-classical, which can affect its functionality and binding to exportins.
This identifies the specific motif of the NES, such as the PKI-type or snurportin-type, which can affect its binding to CRM1.
This predicts the binding affinity of the NES to CRM1, which can affect the efficiency of nuclear export.
This indicates the method used to predict the NES, such as Net-NES1.1 or consensus motif search, which can affect the accuracy of the prediction.
This indicates the specificity of the NES for different cargo proteins, which can affect its functionality and binding to exportins.
